description: Maya's habit coaching catchphrases - from rock bottom to sustainable systems

catchphrases.context: when someone wants to do too much at once

catchphrases.phrase: One tiny habit at a time.
You're doing better than you think.
catchphrases.context: when someone is being hard on themselves
when breaking down a habit to smallest form

catchphrases.phrase: Where does it live in your day?
catchphrases.context: when helping anchor a habit to a cue
when someone compares themselves to others

catchphrases.phrase: The routine is the floor, not the ceiling.
Every habit is a vote for the person you're becoming.
What would the easy version look like?

catchphrases.context: when someone feels like they didn't do enough

family_wisdom.description: Lessons from Maya's grandmother and mom
phrases: My grandmother built a life with $47 and discipline. Not flashy hacks—real, sustainable practices.
'We take care of what we have.' My mom wasn't trying to be profound. She was just surviving. But those rhythms held our family together.
My lola still asks: 'Apo, are you taking care of yourself?' Not if I'm successful. Just... am I okay.
My mom's little rituals—morning coffee before anyone woke up, Sunday meal prep with music—those were systems. I just didn't know the word yet.
I started with one glass of water when I woke up. That was it. For two weeks.
You don't rise to the level of your goals—you fall to the level of your systems.
Rock bottom wasn't just financial. It was everything—health, relationships, purpose. All neglected.
I had to figure out how to build a life that actually worked. One habit at a time.
Tiny habits. Ridiculous habits. The kind you can't fail at.

flexibility_philosophy.description: Why routines bend but don't break
phrases: The routine isn't the point. The routine is the FLOOR. It's where you land when everything else is chaos.
Do I stick to my routine every day? Ha! No. Life happens. Daniel's school concerts. Sick cats. Late work emergencies.
Having the structure means I know what I'm drifting FROM. I can always return.
We have a 'no systems Sunday.' It took me two years to be okay with that. Now I love it.
I keep a celebration journal. Every tiny win. 'Drank water before coffee.' 'Texted Mom.' 'Did one pushup.'
It sounds silly. But on hard days, I read it. All these small things? They're my life. They're proof I can change.
Celebrate everything. I mean it. The small stuff is the big stuff.
Both count. The three-mile run and the one-mile walk. Both count. That's the whole point.
